title: Improvement selection
principles: Every selected improvement must be supported by a traceable causal relationship between the parameter and a demonstrated strength, limitation, or intended function.
Reinforcement strengthens an already supported statistical or mechanical relationship, while friction correction reduces a demonstrated operational limitation.
Parameter direction and improvement purpose must remain separate because increasing, reducing, or otherwise improving a value does not determine why it was selected.
A parameter must be valid for the evaluated attack mode, damage component, and mechanic.
A generally beneficial statistic must not be selected when a more specific supported relationship is absent.
Tradeoffs must remain attached to the candidate they affect and must be considered before assigning priority.
Use none when no allowed candidate has sufficient evidence, causal relevance, and mode compatibility.

reinforce_strength: The candidate directly strengthens a supported statistical or mechanical relationship.
The explanation must identify the relationship being reinforced.
correct_operational_friction: The candidate directly reduces a supported interruption, handling limitation, or contact problem.
The explanation must identify the friction and the causal effect of the parameter.
conditional_improvement: The candidate is beneficial only under a supplied mode, component, activation state, or usage condition.
Preserve that condition in the recommendation.
none: No allowed candidate has enough evidence or semantic compatibility to justify selection.

critical_chance: Select only when increasing critical tier probability directly supports the evaluated critical profile.
Do not treat a lower critical chance relative to multiplier as an automatic deficiency.
critical_multiplier: Select only when additional critical reward directly supports a profile with meaningful critical activation.
Do not select it from multiplier value alone.
status_chance: Select only when eligible status instances and application frequency support additional status probability.
Do not use it to correct contact, reload, or ammunition friction.
base_damage: Select only as an explicitly permitted general offensive reinforcement when no more specific candidate has stronger causal evidence.
Prefer none over base damage when the only justification is that more damage is generally beneficial.
fire_rate: Select only when the supplied mode permits increased attack or application frequency through this parameter.
Preserve tradeoffs involving ammunition pressure, reload frequency, recoil, contact demands, charge timing, burst cycling, or spool-up.
multishot: Select only when the evaluated mode has an explicitly supported multishot interaction.
Do not assume identical effects across projectile, beam, radial, chained, or special components.
magazine_size: Use as reinforcement when a longer active window directly supports the evaluated behavior.
Use as friction correction when frequent depletion is a demonstrated source of reload interruption.
reload_time: Select reduction only when reload downtime is a demonstrated source of friction.
Do not apply conventional reload-time logic to incremental or regenerative recovery without a compatible model.
accuracy: Select only when supplied accuracy evidence identifies a correctable contact limitation.
Do not use accuracy as a substitute for recoil, spread, projectile travel, beam range, or target tracking.
spread: Select reduction only when supplied spread evidence limits valid contact or instance concentration.
Do not infer spread friction from pellet count alone.
recoil: Select reduction only when explicit recoil evidence identifies a repeated control or contact limitation.
Do not infer recoil from fire rate or accuracy values.
multiple_candidates: Order candidates by causal specificity, relevance to the evaluated function, tradeoff severity, and evidence confidence.
Do not treat all eligible candidates as equally important.

exceptions: Do not recommend changing chaining, radial behavior, charge structure, burst count, trigger behavior, or another intrinsic mechanic.
Do not select a parameter absent from allowed_improvement_parameters.
Do not infer strategic relevance merely because a parameter can be numerically improved.
Do not merge recommendations across attack modes or components with different parameter effects.
Do not use a supported role as the sole evidence for selecting a parameter.
